Notice Of Motion To Compel Arbitration And To Dismiss Or In The Alternative, Stay Action Pending Outcome Of Arbitration
Off Calendar Dec-13-2021 Denied
MINI MINUTES:

APPEARANCES (VIA ZOOM VIDEOCONFERENCE):
Atty. MICHAEL M. ASTANEHE, Esq. of Astanehe Law (Tel: 415-226-7170) for plaintiffs;
Atty. CHRISTOPHER D. CHAPLIN, Esq. of Fisher & Phillips LLP (Tel: 213-330-4500) for defendants Shryne Group, Inc., Stiiizy, LLC, and IGS Solutions LLC.

HEARING RE: DEFENDANTS SHRYNE GROUP, INC., STIIIZY, LLC, and IGS SOLUTIONS LLC's Motion To Compel Arbitration And To Dismiss Or In The Alternative, Stay Action Pending Outcome Of Arbitration.

Matter argued and submitted. The Court adopts its tentative ruling.

Defendant IGS Solutions, LLC ("IGS") motion to compel arbitration and stay is denied as to plaintiff Timbal. "Arbitration is?a matter of contract?[and] when presented with a motion to compel arbitration, the court's first task is to determine whether the parties have entered into an agreement to arbitrate their claims." (Avery v. Integrated Healthcare Holdings, Inc. (2013) 218 Cal.App.4th 50, 59; CCP 1281.2 [court determines existence of arbitration agreement].) Here, IGS fails to show that Timbal ever assented to arbitrate her claims. Indeed, the only adduced arbitration agreement is purportedly between IGS and co-employee/plaintiff Mollica. The cases that allow enforcement of an arbitration agreement against a nonsignatory based upon equitable estoppel/intertwining are distinguishable. Timbal and Mollica are co-employees and Mollica is alleged to be a fact witness to Timbal's claims. Their claims, however, are severable and Timbal's claims are not "intimately founded in and intertwined with" Mollica's alleged contract obligations. (Goldman v. KPMG, LLP (2009) 173 Cal.App.4th 209, 221.) "It is the relationship of the claims that is key." (Goldman, 173 Cal.App.4th at 223 (emphasis in original).) Moreover, as explained below, IGS fails to demonstrate that it has an enforceable arbitration agreement with plaintiff Mollica.

IGS' motion to compel arbitration and stay is denied as to plaintiff Mollica. The arbitration agreement does not clearly identify Mollica's employer and the "employer" never even executed the agreement. (Sanchez Decl., Ex. B.) The contract refers to "Retail" as the employer and is on "Shyrne Group, Inc." letterhead. In Flores v. Nature's Best Distribution, LLC (2016) 7 Cal.App.5th 1, the court denied arbitration because, inter alia, the agreement did not sufficiently identify the employer. In this case, the cited agreement suffers from the same defect. The identity of the "employer" is unclear and the agreement even lacks an employer signature block. A valid contract requires identification of all parties to the agreement. (Civil Code 1558; Westlye v. Look Sports, Inc. (1993) 17 Cal.App.4th 1715, 1728 ["It is a basic rule of contract law that 'it is essential to the validity of a contract, not only that the parties should exist, but that it should be possible to identify them.'"].)

Order and Statement of Decision signed.

Judge: Richard B. Ulmer, Jr.; Clerk: Sean Kane; Court Reporter: Maria Torreano, CSR No. 8600, Email: maria.torreano@gmail.com. (302/RBU)
